coredata.dc:title: S, N-doped carbon quantum dots enhanced Luminol-Mn(IV) chemiluminescence reaction for detection of uric acid in biological fluids
coredata.dc:creator: Hallaj, Tooba
Amjadi, Mohammad
Mirbirang, Fatemeh
coredata.dc:description: Herein, S, N doped carbon quantum dots (S, N- CQDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method and characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis. The influence of as-prepared CQDs was studied on the luminol- Mn(IV) CL reaction. The results indicated that S,N-CQDs remarkably increase the CL intensity of this reaction (about 13 fold). Furthermore, it was found that the CL intensity of S,N-CQDs–luminol-Mn(IV) system was significantly declined by uric acid. We exploited the amplified CL system to design a novel probe for the detection of uric acid. The CL intensity of S,N-CQDs–luminol-Mn(IV) reaction was proportional to the logarithm of uric acid concentration in the range of 0.05 to 1.5 µM, with a limit of detection of 17 nM. Based on these results, a sensitive and straightforward CL method was developed for the analysis of uric acid in biological fluids with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Mn (IV)
Sulfur-nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Uric acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of uric acid in real samples
Analytical performance of the CL system for uric acid determination
Uric acid analysis in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image of S,N- CQDs; b) Fluorescence emission spectra of S,N-CQDs excited at 360–480 nm wavelength range, with increments of 20 nm; c) UV-vis spectrum of S,N-CQDs.
a) FT-IR and b) EDS spectrum of S, N-CQDs.
a) Kinetic curve for luminol- Mn(IV) CL system(a) in the absence and (b) presence of S,N-CDs, Condition: Mn(IV) (1.3 × 10−5 M), luminol (1.5 μM), NaOH (7.5 × 10−2 M), 5.0 μL of diluted S,N‐CQDs.

a) CL spectra of luminol- Mn(IV) -S, N-CQDs obtained by the continuous flow of reagents: S, N-CQD solution, luminol (1 × 10−3M and Mn(IV) (5.2 × 10−4 M) in one line and NaOH (1.0 M) in the other line.

(a) Effect of NaOH concentration on the CL signal intensity ratio (I = I2 /I 1), (I1) CL signal intensity in the absence and (I2) presence of S, N-CDs; Condition: Mn(IV) (5.2 × 10−6 M), luminol (1 × 10−6 M), S, N-CDs (10 μL); (b) Effect of luminol concentration on the CL signal intensity ratio; NaOH (0.01 M), other conditions are as in a; (c) Effect of Mn (IV) concentration, luminol (1 × 10−6 M), other conditions are as in b; (d) Effect of S, N-CDs, on the CL intensity. Condition: Mn(IV) (5.2 × 10−6M), other conditions are as in c.

(A) Kinetic curve of luminol- Mn(IV) -S, N-CQDs system (a) alone, (b) in the presence of uric acid (2.5 × 10−7 M) (B) Calibration curve of uric acid; Condition: Mn(IV) (5.2 × 10−6 M), luminol (1 × 10−6 M), NaOH (0.01 M), 15 μl of S, N‐CQDs.
Comparison of the developed method for the determination of uric acid with some reported nanomaterial-based CL methods.
Tolerance limits of some potentially interfering species in the determination of 0.1 µM UA under optimum conditions.

row.entry: Ca2+, Na+, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, Cl −, Ni2+, Sucrose, Glucose, Lactose

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of uric acid in real samples

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: mean of three determinations ± standard deviation. b t-critical = 4.3 for n = 3, P = 0.05.

head.ce:title: S, N-doped carbon quantum dots enhanced Luminol-Mn(IV) chemiluminescence reaction for detection of uric acid in biological fluids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia 5714783734, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCellular and Molecular Research Center, Cellular and Molecular Medicine Institute, Urmia University of Medical Sciences, Urmia 5714783734, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, S, N doped carbon quantum dots (S, N- CQDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method and characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis. The influence of as-prepared CQDs was studied on the luminol- Mn(IV) CL reaction. The results indicated that S,N-CQDs remarkably increase the CL intensity of this reaction (about 13 fold). Furthermore, it was found that the CL intensity of S,N-CQDs–luminol-Mn(IV) system was significantly declined by uric acid. We exploited the amplified CL system to design a novel probe for the detection of uric acid. The CL intensity of S,N-CQDs–luminol-Mn(IV) reaction was proportional to the logarithm of uric acid concentration in the range of 0.05 to 1.5 µM, with a limit of detection of 17 nM. Based on these results, a sensitive and straightforward CL method was developed for the analysis of uric acid in biological fluids with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the last members of carbon nanomaterials family is carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which was reported for the first time in 2004 [1]. After that time, lots of efforts have been made on the creation of new synthesis methods from a verity of materials as a carbon source and development of their applications in different fields [2]. Despite exceptional physicochemical properties such as low toxicity, high chemical stability, biocompatibility, and high resistance to photobleaching, almost all of reported CQDs indicated low quantum yields. The low quantum yield can usually restrict the applicability of CQDs in some fields such as bio-imaging and sensing. One way to overcome this limitation, tune the photoluminescence properties of CQDs and develop their application, is doping CQDs with heteroatoms such as B,N,S, and P [3,4]. For example, synthesis of CQDs with a high quantum yield, around 80%, has been reported by co-doping them with S and N. The S, N-doped CQDs (S, N-CQDs) have been applied in various fields, including chemiluminescence (CL) assays [5].

It should be mentioned that traditional CL reactions usually produce weak CL emission, which makes them unsuitable for the analytical application. Recently, CQDs, including S, N-CQDs, have been attracted remarkable attention as outstanding candidates to introduce novel CL systems with high quantum yield. They have been applied to improve the CL intensity of ultra-weak CL reactions and extend the analytical and bioanalytical applications of traditional CL reactions [6,7]. Direct chemiluminescence reaction of S, N-CQDs with KMnO4, H2O2, and Ce(IV) was reported and applied for the determination of Cu2+, indomethacin, carcinoembryonic antigen, iodide, and mercuric ions, respectively [8,9,10,11]. Moreover, the enhancing effect of S, N‐CQDs was studied on the luminol–H2O2 CL reaction, with application to ranitidine analysis [12].

It is worth mentioning that luminol is the most well-known CL reagent, which is extensively used with diverse CL oxidants including water-soluble MnO2 (Mn(IV)) [13]. Mn(IV), a CL reagent reported by Branett et al. for the first time [14], has been exploited in various CL reactions [15,16]. Herein, the effect of S, N-CQDs as an enhancer was investigated on the luminol-Mn(IV) CL reaction. S, N- CQDs-luminol-Mn(IV) was introduced as a novel CL system and utilized to establish a CL method for analysis of uric acid in the biological fluid. Uric acid is the end product of purine catabolism in the human body. It has been known as a biomarker for various metabolic disorders such as gout, hyperuricemia, kidney failure, von Gierke's disease, Lesch–Nyhan syndrome and so on [17]. Therefore, in the clinical diagnosis of mentioned disorders, it is crucial and essential to monitor amount of uric acid in body fluids. Different electrochemical [18,19,20,21] chromatographic [22,23], fluorescence [24], and chemiluminescence [25,26,27] methods have been established for the determination of uric acid. Lots of these methods have been enzyme-based methods that suffer from some drawbacks such as high cost, special working conditions, poor stability, and reproducibility [28]. The developed method is a nonenzymatic method which possesses general advantages of CL methods such as simplicity of instrumentation, short analysis time, high sensitivity, and low detection limits.

CL measurements were performed on LUMAT LB 9507 chemiluminometer (Berthold; www.berthold.com). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany) was applied to characterize the size and shape of S, N‐CQDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and Cary‐100 spectrophotometer (Varian; www.varianinc.com) were applied for recording UV/vis spectra and FTIR spectrophotometer (Tensor 27, Bruker), respectively. The fluorescence spectra were measured by FP‐8300 spectrofluorimeter (JASCO, Japan) as well as the CL spectrum was obtained by the same instrument with the turned-off excitation light source.

L-Cysteine, citric acid, glucose, urea, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), luminol, potassium permanganate (KMnO4), sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3) and uric acid (UA) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Human plasma samples were obtained from the Blood Transfusion Center (Tabriz, Iran). Doubly distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

The dark-brown solution of Mn(IV) was prepared as described by Perez-Benito et al. [29]. 1.25 mL of 0.1 M KMnO4 solution was added into 100 mL 4.7 × 10−4 M Na2S2O3 with vigorously stirring, then the obtained mixture was transferred to 250 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with deionized water. To confirm the formation of Mn(IV), we recorded UV–visible absorption spectrum of the solution which indicated the characteristic absorption band of Mn(IV) in the range of 200 to 800 nm with a maximum at 375 nm (Fig.S1, Electronic Supporting Information, ESI) [30]. According to the reported extinction coefficient of Mn(IV) (6.336 M −1 cm−1) [31], its estimated concentration was 5.2 × 10−4 M.

A solid-phase hydrothermal method was applied for the preparation of S, N-CDs from citric acid and L-cysteine [32]. After mixing and powdering, the solid mixture of 0.2 g citric acid and 0.1 g L-cysteine, was transferred into a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. A dark-brown syrup was obtained which indicated the formation of CQDs. Subsequently, 1 M NaOH was used to adjust the pH of obtained syrup in 7, and this solution was reached to 50 mL with deionized water. The final solution was diluted 25-fold, and it was stable at 4 °C for several months.

The static injection mode was used for CL analysis. Typically, 15 μL of diluted S, N‐CQDs,10 μL of Mn(IV) (5.2 × 10−4 M), and 10 μL of luminol (10−4 M) were mixed in the 3 mL CL cell. Then, an appropriate volume of sample or standard solution was transferred into the cell, and the volume was adjusted to 900 μL with deionized water. After placing the cell in the instrument, the CL reaction was started by injecting 100 μL of 0.1 M NaOH into the cell. Immediately after injection, the CL intensity was recorded versus time, and the maximum rate of recorded intensity was taken as the CL signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of uric acid in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To precipitate proteins, we added 1.0 mL of methanol to 0.5 mL of plasma in a centrifuge tube. After 20 min centrifuging at 4000 rpm, the obtained clear supernatant was added into a 5.0 mL volumetric flask, and the final volume was adjusted with deionized water. An appropriate portion of this solution was analyzed according to the general procedure.
No pretreatment was necessary for urine samples. Just, 500 μL of 1000-fold diluted urine samples were taken for analysis according to the general procedure.

S, N‐CQDs were synthesized using a simple solid-phase hydrothermal method. TEM image (Fig. 1 a) of S, N-CQDs was taken to determine their size distribution and morphology that indicated they are spherical nanoparticles with size distribution around 5 nm.

One of the popular characterization ways of CQDs is their fluorescent properties, which usually depend on the excitation wavelength. We observed the excitation wavelength-dependent behavior for as-prepared S, N- CQDs. The maximum wavelength of fluorescence emission was ~ 420 nm, with a 360 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 1b). Moreover, as can be seen in UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1c), two absorption characteristic peaks of S, N-CQDs, related to the π–π* and n–π* transition, were appeared at 210 and 350 nm, respectively.

We also studied the FT-IR spectrum of S, N-CQD to ascertain their surface moieties. Our investigation indicated that there are some functional group such as CC, CO, CS, C—N, S–H, O–H and N–H in the surface of S, N-CQDs which their assigned peaks can be observed in 1632, 1711, 1239, 1438, 2466 and 3100 cm −1, respectively (Fig. 2 a). Moreover, the EDS spectrum of prepared CQDs confirms the existence of S and N atoms in these particles with percentage of 2.7 and 16%, respectively (details are given Fig. 2b).

The enhancing effect of various CQDs including glucose derived CQDs (CQDs), graphene quantum dot (GQDs), carbon nitride quantum dots (CN-QDs) and S, N-CQDs, synthesized from diverse carbon and heteroatom sources, was studied on luminol-Mn(IV) CL reaction (Fig. S2, ESI). The greatest enhancing effect was observed for S, N-CQDs. This greater effect can be attributed to the existence of electron-rich heteroatoms such as sulfur and nitrogen in these CQDs, which was known to accelerate some chemical reduction reactions [33,34]. As can be seen from kinetic profiles of the luminol-Mn(IV) reaction, which were recorded in the presence and absences of S, N-CQDs (Fig. 3 ), the intensity of CL system in the presence of S, N-CQDs increased around 13 fold. Furthermore, both CL reactions were so fast, and the maximum CL intensity was achieved within 1 s after injection of NaOH solution. To confirm the enhancing effect of S, N-CQDs, control experiments were performed with a mixture of compounds used in the synthesis of CQDs (L-cysteine and citric acid mixtures). This mixture at the same concentrations used in the synthesis indicated no significant effect on the CL intensity of luminol-Mn(IV) reaction. Furthermore, the direct CL reaction of S, N-CQDs was studied with NaOH and Mn(IV) alone, and both of them together. A negligible CL signal was observed for these reactions.

Then, the enhancing mechanism of S, N- CQDs on luminol- Mn(IV)- S, N- CQDs was studied. According to the literature, Mn(IV) can interact with luminol and elicit CL emission by producing exited 3-aminophthalate. It is worth mentioning that Mn(IV) can be reduced to Mn(III) in alkaline solution, then produced Mn(III) can catalyze the CL reaction [13]. On the other hand, due to electron-donating property of CQDs toward dissolved oxygen in alkaline solution, some radical species, such as OH• and O2 •−, can be produced from the reaction of S, N-CQDs with O2 [35,36]. We suspected that these radical species could interact with Mn(IV) and increase the generation of Mn(III) in the reaction [37]. This, in turn, can enhance the CL intensity of luminol-Mn(IV)-S, N-CQDs reaction.

To confirm these assertions, we did some experiments, such as recording the CL spectra, investigating the effect of some radical scavengers, and removing dissolved oxygen from solutions. As can be observed in Fig. 4 , the CL spectrum of luminol- Mn(IV)-S, N- CQDs reaction appeared in the range of 400–600 nm with a maximum at ~425 nm, which is same as the emission spectrum of 3-aminophethalate, reported in the previous works [38]. This agreement confirms that the final emitting spices in luminol-Mn(IV)-S, N- CQDs reaction is 3-aminophethalate.

Then, the impact of some radical scavengers of oxygen free radicals, such as quercetin, thiourea, glutathione, and DMSO, was examined on the luminol-Mn(IV)-S, N-CQDs CL reaction [39,40]. The results, reported in Table S1 (ESI), verified the effective role of free radicals in luminol-Mn (IV)-S, N-CQDs CL reaction. We also deoxygenated the experimental solutions using bobbling Ar gas inside them and observed the sharp diminution (<95%) in CL intensity. So, the vital role of dissolved oxygen was confirmed in this CL reaction. Based on these facts, the CL mechanism can be written as follows:

To achieve the maximum enchantment effect of S, N-CQDs on the luminol-Mn(IV) CL reaction, we investigated the influence of various factors including the concentration of NaOH, luminol, Mn(VI) and S, N‐CQDs on the CL signal in the presence and absence of S, N‐CQDs (FigS3, ESI). (I = I 2 / I 1 was calculated as an enhancement factor of CL intensity, where I1 and I2 are the CL signals in the absence and presence of S, N‐CQDs, respectively). We also investigated the effect of reagent addition order on the CL intensity (Table S2) and observed that the most intense CL was obtained when NaOH injected lastly.

NaOH concentration impact was studied on the intensifying CL signal in the range of 2.5 × 10 −3 M to 3 × 10−2 M. As shown in Fig. 5 a. the maximum enhancement was obtained at 0.01 M. This concentration was chosen for subsequent experiments.

The effect of luminol concentration in the range of 2 × 10−7 M to 5 × 10−6 M was also investigated on the CL intensity in the presence and absence of S, N –CQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 5b, the highest CL enhancement occurred when 1 × 10−6 M luminol was used. Therefore, this concentration was chosen as an optimum concentration.

The other examined parameter was Mn(IV) concentration, which was studied in the range of 1.0 × 10−6 to 2.6 × 10−5 M (Fig. 5c), the maximum enhancement factor for CL was achieved in 5.2 × 10−6 M of Mn(IV). At higher concentrations, the CL response is probably reduced due to the extinction of CL by excess Mn(IV).

Moreover, amount of S, N-CDs was optimized in the range of 2 to 50 μL. The maximum CL response was acquired at 15 μL, so this value was chosen as the optimal volume (Fig. 5d). However, at higher values, the intensity of CL decreased. This decrease can be attributed to the excess amount of S, N-CDs in comparison to luminol, which may react with Mn(IV) and reduce its amount for reaction with luminol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the CL system for uric acid determination
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of introduced CL system was investigated to design a novel analytical probe for the determination of uric acid. For this purpose, the uric acid effect was examined on the S, N-CQDs -luminol-Mn(IV) CL reaction. We observed that with adding uric acid to this reaction, the CL intensity was decreased (Fig. 6 a). This effect can be assigned to the competition between uric acid and luminol to interact with Mn(IV). It should be mentioned that the UV-vis absorption spectrum of Mn(IV) in the presence and absence of uric acid was measured (Fig.S4a, ESI). The decrease in Mn(IV) absorbance, observed in the presence of uric acid, confirmed interaction between uric acid and Mn(IV). Furthermore, uric acid indicated a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of S, N-CQDs, which confirmed that there is no interaction between uric acid and S, N-CQDs (Fig S4b). Our investigation indicated that under the above mentioned optimal conditions, there is a linear relationship between CL intensity of S,N-CQDs -luminol-Mn(IV) system and the logarithm of uric acid concentration over the range 0.05-1.5 µM, with the detection limit of 17 nM (S/N = 3). The regression equation was ΔI = 851460 LogC + 106 (r2 = 0.9993), where ΔI=I 0-I is the difference between the CL intensity in the presence of uric acid (I) and in its absence (I0), and C is concentration of uric acid (Fig. 6b).

Furthermore, the relative standard deviation for five replicate measurements of 0.25 µM uric acid was obtained to be 1.6%. Some figures of merit of the established method were compared with that of several nanomaterial based CL methods reported for analysis of uric acid. The results summarized in Table 1 . indicate that the introduced method has a lower detection limit compared to most of previous CL methods. Moreover, this method extended the application of heteroatom doped carbon quantum dots in CL systems as well as biological analysis.

To study the selectivity of developed method, we investigated the influence of some species, which may be found in the biological environment, on the determination of uric acid. The tolerance limit for interferences (based on relative error of <5%) is summarized in Table 2 .

As can be seen in Table 2, the most notable interference effects were observed for ascorbic acid, glutathione and Fe(III), which their average concentrations in plasma are < 70 [41], <0.5 [42] and <0.035 μM [43], respectively. These concentrations are lower than that of uric acid in plasma, and also the final concentrations of mentioned species are below their tolerance limits in our method. Therefore, none of these species interferes with uric acid determination. These results confirmed the suitable selectivity of the method for analysis of biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uric acid analysis in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For applicability evaluation of the developed method in real sample analysis, it was applied to control uric acid in human biological fluids such as plasma and urine samples. Additionally, to examine the accuracy of developed method, real samples were spiked with suitable amounts of the uric acid standard solution. Student's t-test analysis confirmed the accuracy of results. Table 3 shows the results of real sample analyses.

In summary, the enhancing effect of S, N-CQDs was examined on the luminol-Mn(IV) CL reaction. Considerable enhancement in CL intensity was observed for this reaction in the presence of S, N- CQDs, so luminol-Mn(IV)- S, N –CQDs was introduced as a novel CL system. The passible CL mechanism was studied by recording the CL spectrum and investigation of the influence of several radical scavengers on the system. The results confirmed that the final emitting species in the system was 3-aminophethalate, whose production was increased in the presence of S, N-CQDs. Concerning the fact that uric acid declines the CL signal of luminol-Mn(IV)- S, N-CQDs reaction, we exploited the introduced system to establish a CL method for the determination of uric acid in the biological fluid.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104841.
